assertion.title: Cube operator + systematic variable permutation templates achieve 50/50 exact matches (up from 39/50)
assertion.statement: Adding a cube unary operator, systematic variable permutation in template seeding, and reducing parsimony coefficient from 0.001 to 0.0001 improves exact matches from 39/50 to 50/50 — a perfect score on all 50 Feynman physics equations.

findings.cube_operator: SUPPORTED — Adding cube(x)=x^3 as a unary operator directly enables eq40 Planck (x^3/(exp(x)-1)) and eq46 Lennard-Jones (via square(square(cube(sigma/r))) for (sigma/r)^12 and square(cube(sigma/r)) for (sigma/r)^6).
findings.systematic_permutation: SUPPORTED — Replacing random rv() variable assignment with itertools.permutations over all equation variables guarantees the correct variable-to-role mapping appears in the initial population. This was the key bottleneck for 4-variable equations (eq18, eq35, eq41, eq47, eq48, eq49) where random assignment had <1% chance of correct mapping per template copy.
findings.reduced_parsimony: SUPPORTED — Reducing PARSIMONY_COEFF from 0.001 to 0.0001 prevents the GP from preferring simpler approximate solutions over exact ones. Critical for eq36 Snell's Law where arcsin(n1*sin(theta1)/n2) (7 nodes, MSE=0) was losing to n1*theta1/n2 (5 nodes, MSE=0.00023) under the old parsimony penalty.
findings.population_increase: SUPPORTED — Increasing POPULATION_SIZE from 500 to 600 accommodates the additional permutation templates (up to 192 for 4-var equations) without crowding out random templates and ramped half-and-half individuals.
